rationale: The page contains a detailed historical timeline of the Indian Civil Services and a comprehensive table of demographic data from the Sample Registration System (SRS) Report 2021, including birth rates, population composition, and mortality rates. This information is highly relevant for both Polity and Social Issues sections of the UPSC exam.
cleanedText:
Historical Evolution of Civil Services in India
- Charter Act, 1853: Ended East India Company’s patronage in recruitment, enjoining recruitment to be through an open competition.
- Lord Macaulay Committee, 1854: Recommended a merit-based competitive examination system.
- First Indian to succeed in ICS: Satyendranath Tagore in 1864.
- Aitchinson Commission, 1887: Recommended reorganization of services into three groups i.e. Imperial, Provincial and Subordinate.
- Government of India Act, 1919: First provided for creation of Public Service Commission and recommended that one-third recruitments to be held in India. From 1922 onwards, exams began to be held in India also (First in Allahabad).
- Lee Commission, 1924: Following its recommendations, first Public Service Commission was established in, 1926, under GoI Act 1919. It was headed by Sir Ross Barker.
- Government of India Act, 1935: Envisaged a Public Service Commission for Federation and a Provincial Public Service Commission. When act came into effect in 1937, Public Service Commission became Federal Public Service Commission (FPSC).
- Constitutional mandate: Upon India’s independence in 1947, FPSC was headed by its first Indian chairman, H.K. Kripalani. With the adoption of Constitution on 26th January 1950, FPSC assumed its present constitutional status (Article 315) as UPSC.
9.4. APPENDIX 4: KEY SAMPLE REGISTRATION SYSTEM (SRS) AND CIVIL REGISTRATION SYSTEM REPORTS DATA
Parameters and Definitions | Trends highlighted by SRS Report 2021
Crude Birth Rate (CBR)
- Definition: CBR = (Number of live births during the year/ Mid-year population) X 100
- Trends:
- DECLINED by 11% from 21.8 in 2011 to 19.3 in 2021.
- States: Maximum: Bihar (25.6); Minimum: Kerala (12.9).
Population Composition (percentage of total population)
- Age group 0-14: Gradual DECLINE from 36.3% (1991) to 24.8% (2021).
- Economically active population (15-59 years): INCREASED (57.7% (1991) to 66.2% (2021)).
- Elderly population: INCREASED (60+ years - 9%; 65+ years - 5.9%).
- Mean age at effective marriage for Females: INCREASED (19.3 years in 1990 to 22.5 years in 2021).
Maternal Mortality Ratio (MMR)
- Definition: Number of maternal deaths during a given period per 100,000 live births during same period.
- Trends:
- DECLINED by 37 points from 130 per lakh live births in 2014–16 to 93 per lakh live births in 2019–21.
- [SDG target of MMR (<=70 by 2030)].
Child Mortality
- Neonatal Mortality Rate (NMR):
- Definition: Number of neonatal deaths from birth to 29 days per 1,000 live births.
- Trends: DECLINED from 26 per 1000 live births in 2014 to 19 per 1000 live births in 2021. [SDG target of NMR (<=12 by 2030)].
- States: Lowest: Kerala; Highest: Madhya Pradesh.
- Infant Mortality Rate (IMR):
- Definition: Number of infants deaths from birth to 1 year old per 1,000 live births.
- Trends: DECLINED from 39 per 1000 live births in 2014 to 27 per 1000 live births in 2021.
- States: Lowest: Kerala (6); Highest: Madhya Pradesh (41).
- Despite this decline, one in every 37 infants at National level die within one year of life.
- Under-five Mortality Rate (U5MR):
- Definition: Number of deaths of children under 5 years of age per 1,000 live births.
- Note: As per Children in India 2025 Report by MoSPI, IMR Decreased from 44 (2011) to 25 (2023).
